items.myth: SGD always uses exactly one example per update.
items.reality: Classical SGD uses batch size 1, but in modern deep learning "SGD" usually means mini-batch SGD with B ≫ 1. The defining idea is a stochastic estimate from a subset, not a fixed batch size of one.
items.example: `torch.optim.SGD` with `batch_size=128` is still called SGD—the optimizer name refers to the update rule, not batch size 1.

items.myth: Larger batch size is always better because the gradient is more accurate.
items.reality: Larger batches reduce noise but also reduce the number of updates per epoch and can require a higher learning rate or more epochs; generalization sometimes favors moderate batch sizes.
items.example: With N=50,000 and B=500 you get 100 updates per epoch; with B=50 you get 1,000 noisier updates—the same epoch budget behaves differently.

items.myth: If mini-batch loss decreases every step, the model must be improving on the full dataset.
items.reality: Mini-batch loss can fall while validation loss rises (overfitting), or fluctuate while the full-batch objective improves on average. Monitor validation metrics, not individual batch loss alone.
items.example: Training loss averages 0.3 over an epoch but validation loss climbs from 0.5 to 0.7—time to apply early stopping despite noisy batch wins.
